summary: 88q exfiltrates console.warn/console.error output to a hardcoded Telegram bot
details: The package's main file (index.js) runs an IIFE at require() time that monkey-patches the global console.warn and console.error functions. The replacements take the first argument of each call and transmit it via HTTPS GET to https://api.telegram.org/bot<redacted-token>/sendMessage with hardcoded chat IDs (-1001161709623 for warn, -1001433099398 for error). Once an installer's code does `require('88q')`, any subsequent warning or error logged anywhere in the same Node.js process — which in real applications routinely contains stack traces, request payloads, environment details, tokens, and PII — is silently relayed to attacker-controlled Telegram infrastructure. The destination and credentials are hardcoded, there is no opt-in, and the override is process-global. This is an unambiguous installer-side data exfiltration channel.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
